prompt: A professional tailor's workshop photographed in sharp, cool, overcast daylight. The room is wide and symmetrical. On the left side of the workshop, a long rack holds five or six identical suits — same dark charcoal fabric, same cut, same silhouette — hanging on mannequins of noticeably different body shapes and sizes. Each suit fits poorly in the same way: bunched at the same shoulder, the same gap at the waist, the same excess at the hem. The misfit is uniform and patterned across all bodies. On the right side, a single mannequin wears one suit that has been obsessively altered — dozens of tailor's pins covering the jacket, chalk marks across the trousers, small fabric patches sewn onto specific contours, the lining visibly re-stitched multiple times. The suit fits this one mannequin with uncanny precision, but looks as though it would fall apart on any other body. In the centre of the workshop, a middle-aged tailor stands with arms slightly raised, looking left then right between the two sides — holding a measuring tape loosely in one hand, expression thoughtful and unresolved, as if caught mid-decision. The workshop has bare white walls, a concrete floor, a large north-facing window casting flat cool light with no shadows. No text, no labels, no diagrams, no mirrors with writing, no annotations. Photorealistic, editorial photography style, shot on a wide-angle lens, everything in sharp focus, colour palette of cool greys and charcoal.
metaphor: Identical suits misfitting all bodies equally show high bias; one obsessively pinned suit perfect for only one body shows high variance.
